verbatim_quote: 19.5 percentage points in 1560, and 17.3 percentage points (excluding the Low Countries) in 1600
reasoning: The distinctive digit-bearing assertions are wrong. Claim states 43.6pp more Protestant by 1560 and at least 29pp by 1600; Rubin's actual figures are 8.8-19.5pp for 1560 and 17.3pp for 1600. Central quantitative claim contradicted by the source, so unsupported.

verbatim_quote: there are positive effects of the BCL on book production and on city growth
reasoning: False unsupported from a retrieval miss. Component 1 (print-adopting cities grew faster after 1500) is proven by the Dittmar evidence sentence. Component 2 (Dutch Brethren of the Common Life built literacy and book-production advantages before Luther) is the entire subject of Akcomak 2016 (BCL founded 14th c., well before Luther) and is directly supported: the paper documents BCL effects on literacy and book production. The 'primed to exploit ideas independent of any single press' clause is reasonable author framing consistent with the source. Both cited findings substantively hold.

verbatim_quote: it was less risky to convert to the Reformation if a strong territory in the immediate neighbourhood had chosen the same path
reasoning: Both components proven in source. Distance to Wittenberg as a major determinant of adoption is stated in the evidence and confirmed as one of the only statistically significant covariates. The covering.uncovered flag ('reform was safer once powerful neighboring states had committed first') is a display artifact: Cantoni states this verbatim via his strategic-neighbourhood theory. Fully supported.

verbatim_quote: Literacy rates in Western European countries during the Middle Ages were below twenty percent of the population.
reasoning: Pipeline already flagged partial_support and it is correct. The Middle Ages <20% component is proven verbatim. The distinctive extension 'into this period' (early modern) is not proven and is in tension with the source's own ~71% illiteracy figure for the 17th century (i.e. ~29% literate, above 20%). Core component proven, distinctive temporal extension unproven -> partial.

verbatim_quote: Ottoman conquests lowered the number of all newly initiated conflicts among the Europeans roughly by 25 percent
reasoning: False unsupported from a retrieval miss (evidence sentence shown was only the intro framing). Iyigun's central finding directly supports the claim: Ottoman military activity reduced intra-European conflict, and conflict between Reformers and Counter-Reformation forces (1520s-1650s, exactly the consolidation decades) depended negatively on Ottoman activity, giving Protestantism room. 'On the Habsburgs' is a minor gloss (the Habsburg-Ottoman axis is the central case) and 'regardless of print' is author framing consistent with the source. Distinctive assertion proven -> supported.
